name: ezi-gex
description: Fast, Unicode-aware regex engine for Zig ŌĆö runtime & comptime, linear-time (ReDoS-safe), full \p{} properties & scripts, SIMD-accelerated. Zero-alloc matching, WASM-ready.
readme:
# ezi_gex

A Unicode-aware regex engine for Zig that runs at **runtime and `comptime`**, with
a **pluggable backend** architecture.

- **Linear-time.** Thompson-NFA based ŌĆö no catastrophic backtracking, ever.
  `(a*)*b` on a long input is fine.
- **Unicode-first.** `\w`, `\b`, `\p{L}`, `\p{Script=Greek}`, case folding, and
  classes are all Unicode-correct. Character **classes** are resolved once into
  sorted code-point ranges ŌĆö matched by a range check, with **no per-character table
  lookup** at match time; `\b` and `\X` consult `ezi_code`'s property tables directly.
  Unicode comes from [`ezi_code`](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-code);
  ezi_gex never touches `std.unicode`.
- **Comptime-capable.** Compile a pattern *and run the match* at compile time ŌĆö
  the program lands in `ro_data`, the matcher runs in `comptime`. (The C++ `ctre`
  trick, in Zig, with full Unicode.)
- **Pluggable.** Matching lives behind a small, vtable-free **backend contract**.
  The library ships seven backends and a dispatcher; you can write your own and
  drop it into the same front door. See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md)
  for the contract, and the step-by-step
  [*write your own backend*](docs/usage-guide.md#8-writing-your-own-backend) walkthrough
  in the usage guide.
- **Target-agnostic.** Pure computation over caller-provided memory ŌĆö no syscalls,
  no global allocator, no platform assumptions in the library code. Imports and
  compiles anywhere Zig (plus `ezi_code`) does: native, `wasm32-freestanding` /
  `wasm32-wasi`, and bare-metal `*-freestanding` (verified to compile for all four).

## Status

**Latest release: `v0.5.1`.** `main` is the active development branch (`0.6.0-dev`); see
[Installing](#installing) for pinning the tag vs. tracking `main`. Pre-1.0, so the API may still
change, but everything in the public surface is annotated `@stable-since: vX.Y.Z` and is covered by
SemVer. Tracks a recent Zig dev build (`0.17.0-dev`); it will not compile on stable 0.16.

**Benchmarked** three ways against **Rust `regex`** and **Go `regexp`** on real
[rebar](https://github.com/BurntSushi/rebar) haystacks ŌĆö reproducible harness (clone + `./run.sh`):
**[github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/regex-bench](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/regex-bench)**.

**What works is tested** ŌĆö **400+ tests** pass (per-module behaviour; cross-backend
conformance, a wide differential corpus where every backend must agree with the Pike VM,
runtime + comptime parity; and a dedicated **ReDoS-immunity suite**, `engine/redos.zig`,
proving the engine is quadratic-immune).

The default `auto` engine is **byte-DFA-first**, with the **eager DFA**
(`engine/backends/edfa.zig`) as the primary span engine: it **fully determinizes the byte
automaton at build time** into a frozen, **Hopcroft-minimized** `states ├Ś byte_classes`
table, so the matcher is a bare table walk with **no per-search state** and runs at
**comptime as well as runtime** (the genuine CTRE-lane ŌĆö `auto` bakes a real frozen DFA into
`ro_data` for tiny patterns at comptime, while a big Unicode class stays on the Pike VM at
comptime but still gets the eager DFA at runtime). Its `find` is **O(input) on every
pattern**, by a static build-time strategy (`program.prone`): an accepting consuming loop
(`\w+`, `\d+`) gets a greedy **anchored restart**; a *prone* pattern (e.g. `\w+@\w+`'s pre-`@`
run) gets the **reverse-DFA two-pass**. The **lazy DFA** (`engine/backends/dfa.zig`) is the
**fallback** when the eager table overflows its state bound. Both are leftmost-first and
conformance-proven against the Pike VM.

Landed across `0.4.0-dev` (all on `main`):

- **`\b`/`\B` word boundaries on the byte DFAs** ŌĆö previously the worst benchmark outlier
  (stuck on the Pike VM, ~10├Ś behind). Distributed: the **eager DFA** bakes **ASCII** word
  boundaries into the frozen table (zero match-time decode); the **lazy DFA** handles full
  **Unicode** boundaries via a *decode-hybrid* (decode only at boundary positions). `auto`
  routes by a cached whole-input ASCII check (ASCII ŌåÆ eager, non-ASCII ŌåÆ lazy), staying
  correct for every input. (`\bthe\b` on Sherlock went from ~89 MiB/s to multi-GiB/s.)
- **`(?m)` line anchors on the eager DFA** ŌĆö `(?m)^`/`(?m)$` run on the DFA (anchored restart
  with line context) for non-prone patterns; a prone `(?m)` falls to the linear Pike VM.
- **One-pass capture fast path** (`backends.onepass`) ŌĆö a single deterministic thread fills
  captures in O(input) for provably one-pass patterns (`(\d{4})-(\d{2})-(\d{2})`,
  `(\w+)@(\w+)`); `auto` uses it for the anchored capture fill after a DFA locates the span.
- **Hopcroft/Moore minimization** of the eager DFA (results-invariant; shrinks the auxiliary
  tables, e.g. `\w+@\w+`'s reverse DFA ~3├Ś fewer states).
- **`$`/`\z` (`text_end`) on the lazy DFA** ŌĆö closing the last eager/lazy capability gap.
- **Two-byte SIMD `memmem` for single literals** (`engine/memmem.zig`) ŌĆö a single literal
  (`Sherlock`, `Sherlock Holmes`) now scans with a portable two-byte filter (probe the two
  *rarest* needle bytes, AND their SIMD equality masks, verify only where both coincide)
  instead of a one-byte memchr. No arch asm (portable `@Vector` compare + movemask). On
  Sherlock this is **17├Ś faster than before and edges out Rust** (40.9 GiB/s). See *Performance*.
- **Whole-run literal `memmem` prefilter** in `auto` ŌĆö the leading-literal start-skip leaps on
  the *whole* literal run (`\bthe\b` jumps "the"ŌåÆ"the", not 't'ŌåÆ't'); a Ōēź2-byte run now rides the
  same **two-byte** `memmem.Finder`, so every prefix-literal NFA/DFA pattern got the upgrade
  (`\bthe\b` on logs **6├Ś**). See *Performance*.
- **Teddy SIMD multi-literal prefilter** ŌĆö literal *alternations* (`cat|dog|fish`,
  `foo|far|fizz`) now scan with the Teddy algorithm: a dynamic in-vector byte shuffle
  (`pshufb`/`vpshufb`/`tbl`) fingerprints the first 1ŌĆō3 bytes of all branches across a 16-byte
  chunk at once, then verifies candidates. Slim (Ōēż8 buckets, 128-bit) everywhere with a native
  shuffle; **fat** (16 buckets, AVX2 256-bit) for large sets. The one piece of arch-specific
  inline asm is quarantined in `engine/simd.zig` with a portable scalar fallback (comptime + any
  unsupported target); the flag `strategy.simd` (`.auto`/`.off`) governs it. Results-invariant.
- **Case-insensitive / small-class ŌåÆ Teddy multi-prefix skip** ŌĆö a pattern that lowers to a short
  run of small classes (`(?i)the` ŌåÆ `[Tt][Hh][Ee]`, `(?i)čćčéąŠ`) gets a synthesised **case-variant
  prefix set** (`(?i)the` ŌåÆ the 8 needles `{THEŌĆ”the}`) that drives the multi-prefix Teddy skip,
  now wired on the **NFA/DFA arm** too (so a top-level alternation like `HolmesŌĆ”|WatsonŌĆ”` gets
  Teddy as well). `(?i)the` ~4.6├Ś, `(?i)sherlock holmes` ~17├Ś, `(?i)čćčéąŠ` ~15├Ś, `near` ~1.6├Ś.
- **Leading-class SIMD scan** (`engine/classscan.zig`) ŌĆö a selective digit/number-class lead
  (`\d+`, `\p{N}+`) SIMD-skips the inter-match gaps to the next class byte, via a **shufti**
  per-high-nibble classifier (exact for Ōēż8 distinct high nibbles, so a sparse scan over non-ASCII
  text pays no false-positive confirms). On sparse-match ASCII corpora `\d+`/`\p{N}+` are
  **~33ŌĆō37├Ś faster (ahead of Rust)**; `\p{N}+` over Cyrillic is **~1.7├Ś faster** since 0.6.0.
- **`(?m)^` line anchors on the lazy DFA** ŌĆö a single leading `(?m)^` runs O(input) on the lazy DFA
  (line-gated forward re-seed + reverse line-accept), with no anchored restart, so a *prone*
  newline-crossing line pattern (`log_line`) is no longer stuck on the Pike VM ŌĆö the quadratic-immune
  complement to the eager DFA's anchored-restart line support.

**New in `0.5.0`** (all results-invariant ŌĆö every path pinned to the Pike VM, runtime + comptime):

- **`\b`-wrapped pure-literal O(1) confirm** ŌĆö when the whole pattern is a literal in word-boundary
  assertions (`\bthe\b`, `the\b`), a `memmem` hit is confirmed by two O(1) boundary checks instead of
  a per-occurrence anchored DFA walk (ASCII on the eager arm, Unicode on the lazy arm, so accented
  prose is fast too). `\bthe\b` over prose **~490 MiB/s ŌåÆ ~3.7 GiB/s (Ōēł8├Ś)**.
- **Fixed-offset interior anchor** ŌĆö when the leading run before an interior literal is fixed-length
  (`\d{4}-ŌĆ”`) and the input is ASCII, the skip jumps anchor-to-anchor and bounded-confirms at the
  pinned start, instead of crawling a dash-dense haystack. `date_iso` **~610 MiB/s ŌåÆ ~9.8 GiB/s (Ōēł16├Ś)**.
- **Line-anchored capture/span dispatch** ŌĆö a `(?m)^ŌĆ”` pattern with no eager DFA attempts the match
  anchored at each line start (for span *and* captures), dropping the lazy DFA's reverse + capture-fill
  passes. `log_line` span **~230 MiB/s ŌåÆ ~1.1 GiB/s (Ōēł4.7├Ś)**.
- **Eager-DFA determinization budget** ŌĆö a big Unicode-class join (`\w+@\w+`, `[\w.+-]+@ŌĆ”`) skips the
  eager DFA (which would burn hundreds of ms, often only to decline) and uses the lazy DFA directly.
  `email` **compile ~0.88 s ŌåÆ ~6 ms (Ōēł140├Ś)**, runtime unchanged.
- **The `\p{ŌĆ”}`/`\w` compile-time cliff is gone** ŌĆö the eager-DFA build is no longer quadratic in a
  Unicode class's size. A class lowers to an `x+` loop over a fan-out split tree, so determinization
  re-closed the loop-back split (re-walking the whole tree, re-interning the same big state) on every
  char-completing edge, and the byte lowering's suffix cache scanned the byte-range DAG linearly per
  tail ŌĆö two independent `O(class┬▓)` hot spots. Both are now linear: a **single-seed closure cache**
  (memoizing `closure([pc])` by `(seed pc, context)`, forward *and* reverse) and a **hashed suffix
  cache**. Compile, `auto` median: `\p{L}+` **31.9 ms ŌåÆ ~1.0 ms (Ōēł32├Ś)**, `\w+` **45 ms ŌåÆ ~0.8 ms
  (Ōēł54├Ś)**, `\b\w+\b` **47.6 ms ŌåÆ ~1.3 ms (Ōēł38├Ś)**, `\p{Lu}\p{Ll}+` **10 ms ŌåÆ ~0.6 ms (Ōēł17├Ś)**. And a
  *prone* pattern with a start-skip prefilter (`the\s+\p{L}+`) **cascades to the lazy DFA** ŌĆö its
  reverse table would be built-but-unused ŌĆö so `the\s+\p{L}+` **compile ~107 ms ŌåÆ ~0.6 ms (Ōēł188├Ś)**.
  Search throughput is unchanged (same automaton; the eager DFA already beats Rust on `\p{L}+`/`\w+`).

`0.2.0`/`0.3.0` foundations still in place: full case folding, grapheme `\X`, a two-tier
`Options` (semantic + results-invariant strategy), `(?x)` verbose mode, ASCII mode,
dead-on-invalid UTF-8, and the **byte-NFA lowering + `ByteMap` equivalence classes**
(`engine/byte.zig`) ŌĆö the zero-decode UTF-8 automaton substrate the DFAs determinize.

## Installing

The latest **tagged** release is **`v0.5.1`** ŌĆö the recommended choice for reproducible
builds. Via git ref (resolves the tag and pins its content hash in `build.zig.zon`):

```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#v0.5.1
```

Or via plain HTTP tarball (also pins the content hash):

```sh
zig fetch --save https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/archive/refs/tags/v0.5.1.tar.gz
```

**Tracking `main` (unreleased `0.6.0-dev`)** ŌĆö if you want the latest in-development surface
before it's tagged, fetch the branch instead of a tag. This resolves `main`'s current commit
and pins its hash in `build.zig.zon`; re-run it to move up:

```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#main
```

`main` is the development branch: it builds and is tested, but APIs there are not yet covered
by a tag, so they can still change before `0.6.0`. For reproducible builds prefer the `v0.5.1`
tag; reach for `main` only when you specifically need unreleased work.

Then in `build.zig` (the `ezi_code` dependency is resolved transitively ŌĆö you only
add `ezi_gex`):

```zig
const ezi_gex = b.dependency("ezi_gex", .{ .target = target, .optimize = optimize });
exe.root_module.addImport("ezi_gex", ezi_gex.module("ezi_gex"));
```

## Quick look

> For the full hands-on tour ŌĆö every op, the pipeline from lexing, comptime paths,
> and writing your own backend ŌĆö see [`docs/usage-guide.md`](docs/usage-guide.md).

```zig
const gex = @import("ezi_gex");

// ŌöĆŌöĆ runtime: compile a (possibly user-supplied) pattern; never crashes ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
var diag: gex.Diagnostic = .{};
var re = gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "(?<user>\\w+)@(?<host>\\w+)", &diag, .{}) catch {
    // diag.message() + diag.faultySlice(pattern) tell you what and where.
    return;
};
defer re.deinit();

// The Scratch is the per-search working state ŌĆö you own it; one per thread.
// Build it directly off the backend's `Scratch` type (heap-backed here); the front
// door never constructs it for you. Reuse one across many searches; never share a
// Scratch across threads.
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);

if (re.find(&sc, "ping bob@example")) |m| {
    _ = m.slice("ping bob@example"); // "bob@example"
}

// captures: provide a slots buffer of re.slotCount() == 2*(groups+1)
const slots = try gpa.alloc(?usize, re.slotCount());
defer gpa.free(slots);
if (re.captures(&sc, slots, "bob@example")) |c| {
    _ = c.namedSlice("user"); // "bob"
    _ = c.namedSlice("host"); // "example"
}

// ŌöĆŌöĆ comptime: program baked into the binary; match runs at compile time ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("(\\d{4})-(\\d{2})", .{});
const yes = comptime Re.isMatchComptime("y2026-06"); // true, computed at build
const cap = comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06").?; // groups resolved at build (usable from v0.2.0-dev)
const year = comptime cap.groupSlice(1).?;              // "2026", a ro_data slice (usable from v0.2.0-dev)
```

> ### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Comptime has limits ŌĆö and the trade-off is yours
>
> `compileComptime` runs the **entire** parse ŌåÆ HIR ŌåÆ program lowering inside the Zig
> compiler's const-evaluator and bakes the result into `ro_data`. Two consequences you own
> as a deliberate choice ŌĆö the library will not decide them for you:
>
> 1. **It only works until the compiler runs out of room.** Const-eval is bounded by the
>    eval-branch quota and compiler memory; a large, deeply-nested, or pathological pattern
>    can blow the quota or make builds slow and memory-heavy. **Prefer `compileRuntime` for
>    big or user-supplied patterns** ŌĆö runtime compilation has no such ceiling.
> 2. **Each comptime program adds *its* ranges to `ro_data`.** A `compileComptime` regex
>    bakes its class ranges in: ~6.3 KB per *distinct* `\w` (~800 ranges), ~5.3 KB per
>    `\p{L}`, Ōēż0.5 KB for ASCII classes. **Identical classes within a pattern are interned
>    to one range-block**, so a counted repeat like `\w{3,32}` costs *one* `\w`, not one
>    per copy ŌĆö repetition no longer multiplies the table. The cost is one block per
>    *distinct* class per comptime pattern; `compileRuntime` adds nothing to the binary.
>    The Unicode *tables* themselves are a fixed one-time cost, **not** per-pattern ŌĆö see
>    [┬¦ Binary size](#binary-size). Stacking many *different* Unicode classes across many
>    comptime programs is the only thing that grows `ro_data`; check the delta then.

## Matching & captures

Everything below uses the default `auto` engine ŌĆö `compileRuntime` /
`compileComptime`. The shape never changes: **compile once ŌåÆ make a `Scratch` ŌåÆ
run searches**. The compiled regex is immutable and shareable; the `Scratch` is the
single piece of mutable per-search state.

> Prefer copy-paste? The usage guide has a runnable recipe for each op below in
> [┬¦3 Front-door recipes](docs/usage-guide.md#3-front-door-recipes), plus the
> [`Options`](docs/usage-guide.md#options) reference and the
> [comptime / no-allocator](docs/usage-guide.md#4-comptime--no-allocator-usage) paths.

### 1. The `Scratch` ŌĆö the engine only needs *a* scratch

The engine is **`Scratch`-type agnostic.** Every search op takes a
`*@TypeOf(re).Scratch`, and that is the *entire* requirement. The front door never
constructs it, never stores an allocator for it, and assumes **nothing** about what it
holds ŌĆö whether a `Scratch` is heap-allocated, carved from a caller buffer, stateless
(`struct{}`), or something exotic is **purely the backend's design**. Buffer
semantics, allocator semantics, comptime-ability: all optional, all the backend's
call. `Compiled` holds only the `Scratch` *type* and forwards your `&sc` straight
through to the backend ŌĆö so you build the `Scratch` yourself, directly off
`@TypeOf(re).Scratch`, threading in `&re.program`:

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "[a-z]+\\d+", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();

// Heap-backed ŌĆö every built-in backend's Scratch defines `init` / `deinit`.
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);
```

If the backend implements the **buffer convention** (its `Scratch` exposes
`Buf` / `bufferLen` / `initBuffer` ŌĆö every built-in except the runtime-only lazy `dfa`
does), you can hand it caller-owned storage instead, with no allocator and no allocation
*during* a search:

```zig
// Fixed buffer ŌĆö `bufferLen` reports how many `Buf` words this program needs.
const buf = try gpa.alloc(@TypeOf(re).Scratch.Buf, @TypeOf(re).Scratch.bufferLen(&re.program));
defer gpa.free(buf);
var sc_buf = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.initBuffer(buf, &re.program);

// For a comptime regex the length is comptime-known ŌåÆ a stack array, no allocator:
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("[a-z]+\\d+", .{});
var stack_buf: [@TypeOf(Re).Scratch.bufferLen(&Re.program)]@TypeOf(Re).Scratch.Buf = undefined;
var sc_ct = try @TypeOf(Re).Scratch.initBuffer(&stack_buf, &Re.program);
```

A backend with a different construction protocol is built however *it* specifies ŌĆö for
a stateless one that is simply `var sc: @TypeOf(re).Scratch = .{};`. Whatever the
backend's choice, you end up with a value the engine accepts.

> The front door dictates no representation and reaches for no scratch method on the
> runtime path: it cares that a `Scratch` *value* exists, not how it was made. (The
> comptime helpers ŌĆö `isMatchComptime`/`findComptime`/ŌĆ” ŌĆö are the one exception: with
> no allocator in const-eval they carve a buffer `Scratch` inline, so there they do
> require the backend's buffer convention.)

### 2. Searching ŌĆö `isMatch`, `find`, `findAll`, `count`, `split`

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "\\w+", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);

const text = "the quick brown fox";

_ = re.isMatch(&sc, text);                 // true
const m = re.find(&sc, text).?;            // first match (leftmost)
_ = m.start;  _ = m.end;  _ = m.slice(text); // "the"

_ = re.count(&sc, text);                   // 4 ŌĆö non-overlapping matches

var it = re.findAll(&sc, text);            // iterate every match
while (it.next()) |hit| _ = hit.slice(text); // "the","quick","brown","fox"

var parts = re.split(&sc, "a, b ,c");      // split on the pattern
while (parts.next()) |piece| _ = piece;    // "a, b ,c" split on /\w+/ ŌåÆ punctuation/spaces
var head = re.splitN(&sc, "a,b,c,d", 2);   // at most 2 pieces: "a", then "b,c,d"
```

The `*At` variants take `SearchOptions` to resume/anchor: `isMatchAt`, `findAt`, `capturesAt`.

Matching is **leftmost-first** (Perl/JS), linear-time, and Unicode-correct. `find`
returns `null` (not an error) when there is no match.

### 3. Captures ŌĆö numbered and named groups

`captures` resolves the first match's submatches into a `slots` buffer you provide.
Size it with `re.slotCount()` (`== 2 * (groups + 1)` ŌĆö two offsets per group plus the
whole match at index 0). At **runtime** the group count is dynamic, so allocate; at
**comptime** it is known, so a stack array works.

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "(?<user>\\w+)@(?<host>\\w+)", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);

const slots = try gpa.alloc(?usize, re.slotCount()); // 2 * (2 groups + 1) = 6
defer gpa.free(slots);

if (re.captures(&sc, slots, "ping bob@example")) |c| {
    _ = c.match().slice("ping bob@example"); // "bob@example"  (group 0)
    _ = c.groupSlice(1).?;                    // "bob"          (by number)
    _ = c.namedSlice("user").?;               // "bob"          (by name)
    _ = c.namedSlice("host").?;               // "example"
    _ = c.group(2);                           // ?Match for group 2, or null if absent
}
```

A group that did not participate reads back `null` (`groupSlice`/`namedSlice` return
`null`, never stale data). To stream captures over every match, use `capturesAll`
(it reuses one `slots` buffer ŌĆö each `Captures` is valid only until the next
`next()`):

```zig
var cit = re.capturesAll(&sc, slots, "a@b x@y");
while (cit.next()) |c| _ = c.namedSlice("user"); // "a", then "x"
```

Map group names Ōåö indices straight from the compiled pattern (no match needed) with
`re.groupIndex("user")` (ŌåÆ `?usize`) and `re.groupName(1)` (ŌåÆ `?[]const u8`).

### 4. Replace ŌĆö templates, counts, an owned string, or a callback

`$0`/`$&` is the whole match, `$1`/`${name}` reference groups, `$$` is a literal `$`.
There's a `Writer`-based form, a count-bounded form, an **allocating** form, and a
**callback** form:

```zig
var re = try gex.compileRuntime(gpa, "(\\w+)@(\\w+)", &diag, .{});
defer re.deinit();
var sc = try @TypeOf(re).Scratch.init(gpa, &re.program);
defer sc.deinit(gpa);
const slots = try gpa.alloc(?usize, re.slotCount());
defer gpa.free(slots);

// (a) into a Writer:
var out: std.Io.Writer.Allocating = .init(gpa);
defer out.deinit();
try re.replaceAll(&sc, "bob@example", "$2/$1", slots, &out.writer); // "example/bob"

// (b) get an owned []u8 directly ŌĆö no Writer to build:
const s = try re.replaceAllAlloc(gpa, &sc, "bob@example", "$2/$1", slots); // "example/bob"
defer gpa.free(s);

// (c) bounded: only the first match (`replace`), or the first n (`replaceN(..., n)`).
try re.replace(&sc, "a@b c@d", "<$1>", slots, &out.writer); // first match only

// (d) callback ŌĆö compute each replacement from the captures:
try re.replaceAllWith(&sc, "a@b c@d", slots, &out.writer, {}, struct {
    fn run(_: void, c: gex.Captures, w: *std.Io.Writer) std.Io.Writer.Error!void {
        for (c.groupSlice(1).?) |ch| try w.writeByte(std.ascii.toUpper(ch)); // upper-case the user
    }
}.run);
```

Replace is **fast by default**: a template that references no group (a constant, or only
`$0`) runs at span-search speed (the DFA), skipping the capture engine entirely; only
`$1`+/`${name}` templates pay for captures.

### 5. The same calls at comptime

When the whole regex *and* the input are known at compile time, the match runs in
const-eval ŌĆö no `Scratch` to manage, no allocator. The results are baked into
`ro_data` and are usable at runtime as well:

```zig
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("(\\d{4})-(\\d{2})", .{});

const ok   = comptime Re.isMatchComptime("y2026-06"); // true
const m    = comptime Re.findComptime("y2026-06").?;  // whole match
const n    = comptime Re.countComptime("2026 2027");  // 2
const c    = comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06").?;
const year = comptime c.groupSlice(1).?;              // "2026" ŌĆö by number
```

**Comptime named captures.** Named groups resolve at compile time exactly like at
runtime ŌĆö `namedSlice` works inside const-eval, and the result is a `ro_data` slice
you can keep as a plain `const`:

```zig
const Re = comptime gex.compileComptime("(?<year>\\d{4})-(?<month>\\d{2})", .{});

if (comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06")) |c| {
    const year  = comptime c.namedSlice("year").?;  // "2026"
    const month = comptime c.namedSlice("month").?; // "06"
    _ = year;  _ = month;
}

// ŌĆ”or pull one straight out as a compile-time constant baked into the binary:
const year = comptime Re.capturesComptime("y2026-06").?.namedSlice("year").?; // "2026"
```

## Backends

| Backend | Strategy | Captures | Comptime | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `auto` *(default)* | dispatches the others | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | just use this |
| `pikevm` | breadth-first NFA | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | general, large inputs; Unicode `\b` |
| `backtrack` | bounded depth-first NFA | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | small inputs; the only `\X` backend |
| `literal` | substring (two-byte SIMD **`memmem`**) / literal-alternation (SIMD **Teddy**) | whole-match | Ō£ģ | pure-literal patterns |
| `onepass` | single deterministic NFA thread | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | provably one-pass capture fill (anchored) |
| `bytepike` | byte-stepping Pike VM (zero-decode) | Ō£ģ | Ō£ģ | byte-automaton substrate; ASCII `\b`, no `\X` |
| `edfa` *(default span engine)* | **eager** DFA ŌĆö frozen `states ├Ś byte_classes` table | span-only | Ō£ģ | fast O(n) span scan; ASCII `\b`, `(?m)`, `$`/`\z` |
| `dfa` | lazy DFA over the byte automaton (cached transitions) | span-only | Ō£Ś (runtime-only) | fallback when the eager table overflows; Unicode `\b` |

`compileRuntime`/`compileComptime` use `auto`, which **prefers the eager DFA** (`edfa`) for
the span scan, falls back to the lazy `dfa` when the eager table overflows its state bound,
then to the NFA. Captures are filled **anchored at the DFA span** by `onepass` (for one-pass
patterns) or the Pike VM. `auto` routes feature by feature and **never `@compileError`s** ŌĆö it
is correct for every pattern and input: ASCII `\b` and non-prone `(?m)` ride the **eager** DFA,
**Unicode `\b`** (non-ASCII input) the **lazy** DFA, and `\X` / a prone `\b` or `(?m)` / a mixed
`$` stay on the code-point Pike VM. The DFA is **on by default** (`byte_engine = .auto`/
`.enabled`); `.disabled` opts back to the NFA-only program. Force a specific backend with the
`*With` variants: `gex.compileRuntimeWith(gex.backends.pikevm, gpa, pat, &diag, .{})`.

A **single** literal (`Sherlock`) routed to `literal` is scanned with a portable two-byte SIMD
**`memmem`** (`engine/memmem.zig`): probe the two rarest needle bytes, AND their `@Vector` equality
masks across a 16/32-byte chunk, verify only where both coincide ŌĆö no arch asm (lowers to SSE2/NEON
everywhere). A literal **alternation** (`cat|dog|fish`) instead uses the **Teddy** SIMD prefilter on
a target with a native dynamic shuffle (x86-64 SSSE3/AVX2, aarch64 NEON) ŌĆö fingerprint all branches
across a 16-byte chunk at once, then verify. Slim (Ōēż8 buckets) by default; **fat** (16 buckets) on
AVX2 for larger sets; portable scalar fallback at comptime and on other targets. Both are governed by
`strategy.simd` (`.auto`/`.off`) ŌĆö a *permission*, not a command: there is no way to force SIMD onto
a target that lacks it, so the result is always correct.

The usage guide covers [choosing a backend](docs/usage-guide.md#choosing-a-specific-backend)
and walks the whole [*write your own backend*](docs/usage-guide.md#8-writing-your-own-backend)
process end to end.

## When to use comptime ŌĆö honest advice

The library has two things it calls "comptime" and they behave quite differently, so
it's worth being clear about which one you actually want.

**`compileComptime` ŌĆö program in ro_data, match at runtime.** The full compile pipeline
runs in the Zig const-evaluator and the result is baked into the binary. Matching still
happens at runtime over runtime input. You use it exactly like a `compileRuntime` regex
once compiled.

**`*Comptime` methods (`isMatchComptime`, `findComptime`, `capturesComptime`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö both
pattern and input are compile-time constants.** The entire match runs during the build
and the result is a compile-time constant. These are a different beast.

### For most code: just use `compileRuntime`

Pattern compilation is a one-time cost ŌĆö microseconds to low milliseconds, paid once at
startup and never again. There is no meaningful performance argument for `compileComptime`
over `compileRuntime` followed by runtime matching. The compiled regex is immutable; you
compile once, store it somewhere, and reuse it for the lifetime of the process.

`compileRuntime` also has cleaner error handling (a `Diagnostic` with a caret, not a
`@compileError` buried in a stack of const-eval frames), no binary-size overhead, and no
compiler eval-quota ceiling. It should be your default for nearly everything ŌĆö fixed
patterns included.

### When `compileComptime` is actually worth it

Use it when **all three** are true:

1. The pattern is fixed in source (not user-supplied, not assembled at runtime).
2. You specifically want the build-time guarantee ŌĆö a typo in the pattern is a
   `@compileError`, not a runtime error path you have to handle.
3. The pattern is light on Unicode classes.

That third one is the catch ŌĆö though a much smaller one than it used to be. This engine
resolves Unicode classes to sorted code-point ranges at HIR time, and a
`compileComptime` program bakes its ranges into `ro_data`. One `\w` is ~6.3 KB
(802 ranges ├Ś 8 B), `\p{L}` ~5.3 KB (684 ranges), `\d` ~0.6 KB (72 ranges), `\s` ~80 B (10 ranges). **Identical classes
inside a pattern are interned to a single range-block**, so a counted repeat like
`\w{3,32}` costs *one* `\w` (~6.3 KB), not one per copy ŌĆö repetition no longer
multiplies the table. The remaining cost is one block per *distinct* class per
comptime pattern. If your pattern is ASCII-heavy (`\d`, `[a-z]`, explicit ranges) the
cost is negligible; if it stacks many *different* Unicode classes, check the delta.
See [┬¦ Binary size](#binary-size) for the whole picture (the Unicode tables themselves
are a fixed one-time cost, not a per-pattern one).

### When the `*Comptime` match methods make sense

These require the input to be a compile-time constant, which makes them self-selecting:
if your data is dynamic, you simply cannot use them. That rules out essentially all
production matching code.

They genuinely shine as build-system tools: asserting at build time that a constant
string matches an expected format (`comptime assert(Re.isMatchComptime(EXPECTED))`),
generating a lookup table from a constant corpus, or pulling a named group out of a
source-level constant as its own typed constant. The results are baked in and cost
nothing at runtime. Just keep the input short ŌĆö the eval-branch quota scales with input
length, and a large string in const-eval will slow your build.

## Thread-safety

The `Program` is `*const` during a search ŌĆö compiler-enforced immutable, so a single
`Compiled`/`Program` is freely shareable across threads with no locks, no atomics, no
global state. The `Scratch` is the only mutable per-search state, and **how it behaves
under concurrency is the backend's choice** ŌĆö a backend is free to make its `Scratch`
thread-safe. The built-in backends do **not**: their `Scratch` is mutated on every
search, so give **each thread its own** (never pool one across threads).

Two facts about the built-ins, both stemming from the *caller-supplied* allocator
rather than any hidden internal one ŌĆö the front door allocates nothing during a search:

- The `backtrack` heap `Scratch` (which `auto` uses for small inputs) grows its visited
  set on demand **through the allocator you passed to `Scratch.init`**. So if several
  threads' scratches share one *non-thread-safe* allocator, two growing at once race
  inside that allocator ŌĆö give each thread its own allocator, or a thread-safe one.
- A **buffer-backed `Scratch`** (`initBuffer`) and the **`pikevm`** backend allocate
  nothing at all while matching, sidestepping that entirely.

Full details in [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) ┬¦11 and the usage guide's
[┬¦9 Thread-safety](docs/usage-guide.md#9-thread-safety).

## Performance

> **Benchmark:** the numbers below come from a like-for-like, three-way throughput +
> compile-time comparison against **Rust `regex`** and **Go `regexp`** on byte-identical
> [rebar](https://github.com/BurntSushi/rebar) haystacks. The harness is a separate,
> reproducible repo ŌĆö clone it and run `./run.sh`:
> **[github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/regex-bench](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/regex-bench)**
> (it fetches this engine from GitHub, so anyone can reproduce the comparison).

Honest about where it stands. **Tier 1 ŌĆö the literal / prefilter fast path ŌĆö is now
wired:**

1. The `literal` backend scans a **single** literal (`Sherlock`, `Sherlock Holmes`) with a
   portable **two-byte SIMD `memmem`** (`engine/memmem.zig`): probe the two *rarest* needle
   bytes, AND their SIMD equality masks across a 16/32-byte chunk, and verify only where both
   coincide ŌĆö far fewer candidates than a one-byte memchr on a common lead byte. No arch asm
   (portable `@Vector` compare + movemask, lowering to SSE2/NEON everywhere). On Sherlock this
   is **17├Ś** the old `std.mem.indexOf` scan and **edges out Rust** (40.9 GiB/s). A literal
   **alternation** (`cat|dog|fish`) uses the **Teddy** SIMD prefilter instead: a dynamic
   in-vector byte shuffle (`pshufb`/`vpshufb`/`tbl`) fingerprints the first 1ŌĆō3 bytes of all
   branches across a 16-byte chunk at once (slim Ōēż8 buckets; **fat** 16 buckets on AVX2), then
   verifies candidates ŌĆö far more selective than one `indexOfAny` when branches share a first
   byte. Teddy's one bit of arch-specific asm is quarantined in `engine/simd.zig` (portable
   scalar fallback at comptime / on other targets); both toggle with `strategy.simd`.
2. `auto` consumes the HIR [`Analysis`](docs/usage-guide.md#7-the-analysis-prefilter-facts)
   on NFA *and* DFA patterns: when every match must begin
   with a fixed literal, the **whole leading literal run** drives a SIMD `memmem`-style skip
   that leaps straight to each candidate start ŌĆö literal-to-literal, not byte-to-byte
   (`\bthe\b` jumps "the"ŌåÆ"the" instead of 't'ŌåÆ't', skipping every interior "the"). A Ōēź2-byte
   run rides the same **two-byte** `memmem.Finder` as item 1 (AND the SIMD masks of the run's
   two rarest bytes, verify only where both coincide) ŌĆö deliberately **not** `std.mem.indexOfPos`,
   which falls back to a non-SIMD scan for needles Ōēż 4 bytes (the common "the"/"http" sizes). Each
   candidate is confirmed with an *anchored* run; a `^`/`\A` pattern
   skips the leftward scan entirely; the **rarest required byte** drives a sound
   fast-reject (no `@` in the input ŌćÆ no `\w+@\w+` match, return at once); and a
   min-length gate rejects inputs too short to hold any match. Every `Analysis` fact is a
   sound one-sided bound, so the prefilter never drops a real match ŌĆö it only avoids
   running the engine where one cannot start. Toggle with `strategy.prefilter`.

**Tier 3 ŌĆö the byte DFA ŌĆö is wired and now ON BY DEFAULT, with the *eager* DFA as the
primary span engine** (`backends/edfa.zig`, reached through `auto`). `auto` builds the eager
DFA ŌĆö a fully-determinized, **Hopcroft-minimized** frozen `states ├Ś byte_classes` table ŌĆö and
uses it for `isMatch`/`find` on most patterns, including **ASCII `\b`** and non-prone **`(?m)`**
line anchors and trailing **`$`/`\z`**; only `\X`, a prone `\b`/`(?m)`, or a mixed `$` fall to
other arms. Captures are filled **anchored at the DFA span** by `onepass` (one-pass patterns)
or the Pike VM. The lazy DFA (`backends/dfa.zig`) is the **fallback** ŌĆö when the eager table
overflows its state bound, and the arm that carries **Unicode `\b`** (decode-hybrid) on
non-ASCII input. The default is **at Rust parity (~1.1├ŚŌĆō1.3├Ś) on every class scan** and **Ōēź the
code-point Pike VM in every cell** (5ŌĆō10├Ś on character-class scans), it is **results-invariant**
(`conformance.zig` pins span and captures to the Pike VM and fuzzes the strategy knobs), and
**quadratic-immune** (`engine/redos.zig`). `strategy.byte_engine = .disabled` opts back to the
compact NFA-only program.

**`find` is O(n) on every pattern ŌĆö via a build-time strategy choice.** `computeProne`
detects, at build, whether the anchored DFA has a **non-accepting cycle reachable from a
start** (it can consume an unbounded run without ever accepting):

- **Non-prone** (`\w+`, `\d+`, `[A-Za-z]+` ŌĆö the consuming loop is itself accepting) ŌåÆ
  **anchored restart**: one greedy frozen-table walk per match. O(input) because no start
  can scan far without hitting an accepting state (~1.1 GiB/s).
- **Prone** (`\w+@\w+` on long word runs, `[ab]*c`) ŌåÆ the **reverse-DFA two-pass**: a
  forward one-pass (`utrans` `.*?`-prefix table) locates the match **end**, a frozen
  **reverse** DFA (determinized at comptime *and* runtime) the leftmost **start**. O(input),
  replacing the old ╬ś(n┬▓) anchored restart on this begins-everywhere-completes-rarely class.

There is **no per-search probing** ŌĆö the arm is fixed at build. A **trailing-`$`** pattern
(`\w+@\w+$`, `[ab]*c$`, `\w+$`: every match ends at input end) takes a third O(input) arm ŌĆö
the end is pinned to `input.len`, so a single **reverse-DFA pass from the end** finds the
leftmost start (no anchored restart, which would be ╬ś(n┬▓) on these begin-but-don't-complete
shapes). The eager DFA also **builds only the tables it will use**: `utrans` and the reverse
table are built **only for prone or trailing-`$` patterns**, so a non-prone `\w+` stores just
its forward `trans` table (~141 KB), not the full forward + `.*?`-prefix + reverse trio
(which for a prone pattern like `\w+@\w+` totals ~1 MB). Leftmost-first,
conformance-pinned to the Pike VM.

Since first writing this, most items here have **landed**: `\b`/`\B` and `(?m)` on the byte DFAs,
a one-pass capture backend (`onepass`), DFA **Hopcroft minimization**, `$`/`\z` on the lazy DFA,
the whole-run literal `memmem` start-skip, the **two-byte SIMD `memmem`** for single literals
(Sherlock now *faster than Rust*), and the **Teddy SIMD multi-literal prefilter** for alternations
(the single biggest remaining gap vs. Rust). **`0.5.0`** then closed the inner-literal, word-boundary
and line-anchored cases: a **`\b`-wrapped pure-literal O(1) confirm** (`\bthe\b` over prose ~8├Ś), a
**fixed-offset interior anchor** (`\d{4}-ŌĆ”` ~16├Ś), **line-anchored capture/span dispatch** for
`(?m)^ŌĆ”` (`log_line` ~4.7├Ś), and an **eager-DFA determinization budget** (email compile ~0.88 s ŌåÆ
~6 ms). The **multi-prefix Teddy** on the NFA/DFA arm and a **case-variant set** for `(?i)`/small-class
leads landed in 0.4.0.

**Still open (additive, no API change):** the remaining gaps vs. Rust are now concentrated in
**dense Unicode-class DFA throughput** ŌĆö patterns whose match runs are themselves the whole input,
so no prefilter can skip and the determinized table walk is the work (`\p{L}+`, `\p{Lu}\p{Ll}+`/
`cap_word`, `[A-Za-z]+`). The next levers are a **faster inner DFA loop** and a **sparse DFA table
encoding** (the kept Unicode-class tables are dense). See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) ┬¦10.

## Binary size

Class **matching** is delegated to `ezi_code`'s **enumerable range tables** ŌĆö the HIR
resolves every class (`\d \w \s`, `\p{ŌĆ”}`, scripts, `[...]`) to sorted code-point ranges,
matched by a range check with **no per-character table lookup**. The Unicode **assertions**
are the exception, and contribute the bulk of the linked Unicode data: match-time `\b`/`\B`
(Unicode) tests word-ness via `ezi_code`'s **DerivedCoreProperties** table, `\X` via the
**grapheme-break** table, and full `(?i)` folding via the **case-fold** tables ŌĆö so those are
linked too (`ezi_gex` still links none of `ezi_code`'s per-code-point *General_Category* trie).

Either way the cost is **fixed, not growing** ŌĆö the tables are shared and linked once, the same
whether you compile 1 pattern or 10 000:

| What | Cost | Grows withŌĆ”? |
|---|---|---|
| `ezi_code` Unicode tables linked into the demo ŌĆö class **ranges** (`category_runs`, `derived_runs`, `script_runs`) **plus** the assertion **tables** (DerivedCoreProperties for `\b`, grapheme-break for `\X`, case-fold for `(?i)`) | **Ōēł 385 KB, linked once** *(measured by symbol span; largest: DerivedCoreProperties ~161 KB, case-fold ~95 KB, category ranges ~49 KB, derived ranges ~40 KB)* | **nothing** ŌĆö same for 1 pattern or 10 000 |
| a runtime-compiled regex (`compileRuntime`) | on the **heap**, not the binary | the pattern |
| a comptime-compiled regex (`compileComptime`), per pattern | one program in `ro_data`; ~6.3 KB per *distinct* `\w`, ~5.3 KB per `\p{L}`, Ōēż0.5 KB for ASCII classes | the pattern's distinct classes |

So once a program touches the regex engine at all, the Unicode tables are paid for **once** and
never again ŌĆö adding more patterns, longer patterns, or more Unicode classes cannot push that
~385 KB any higher (and a program that uses *no* Unicode assertions and only ASCII classes pulls
far less). The only size that scales with your code is `ro_data` for the `compileComptime`
programs you choose to bake in, and even there **identical classes are interned** (a class used N
times in one pattern is stored once; `\w{3,32}` costs one `\w`, not 35). `compileRuntime` adds
nothing to the binary beyond the shared tables.

> **The byte engine** (on by default; `byte_engine = .disabled` opts out) builds a
> separate *byte* automaton on the **heap** ŌĆö never linked into the binary ŌĆö and it is
> larger than the code-point program because a Unicode class becomes automaton structure
> rather than a flat table. Two passes keep it bounded: **UTF-8 suffix sharing** (a
> `(lo,hi,next)` cache that emits each class's shared continuation tails once) and
> **single-copy `x+`** compilation. Measured: `\w+` is ~3.9 k instructions (down from
> ~11 k), `\w+@\w+` ~7.9 k (down from ~23 k); ASCII patterns are unchanged. A
> `byteWorthLowering` cost-gate keeps a pathological pattern (a big class repeated dozens
> of times) on the compact code-point engine entirely. The **lazy** DFA (the fallback)
> never builds the whole automaton ŌĆö it materializes only the states an input visits (a
> handful over ordinary text), so the NFA's bulk is build-time, not per-search, memory.
> The **eager** DFA (`backends.edfa`, the default span engine) *does* freeze the whole DFA
> into a `states ├Ś byte_classes` table ŌĆö into `ro_data` at comptime ŌĆö and is also the
> CTRE/bake-it-in path: tiny for literal/ASCII patterns (`abc` is 5 states / ~100 B), a
> few hundred states for a Unicode class. It now **builds only the tables it uses** (a
> non-prone `\w+` keeps just its forward table, ~141 KB, not the forward + `.*?`-prefix +
> reverse trio ŌĆö which for a prone pattern like `\w+@\w+` totals ~1 MB). See [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md) ŌåÆ *The byte
> substrate* / *The eager DFA*.
>
> **Build-time note ŌĆö determinization is ~O(states) (hash-interned).** The forward and reverse
> determinizers intern DFA states through an open-addressing hash, so building a *big Unicode
> class* DFA is ~linear in its state count. (Was an O(states┬▓) scan ŌĆö `\w+@\w+`, `\w+@\w+$`,
> `\p{L}+$` took ~seconds to *compile*; now milliseconds.) It is a **one-time build cost; match
> time is O(input), unaffected.** ASCII-class and literal patterns build instantly.

Reference point ŌĆö the bundled `main.zig` demo (which exercises runtime *and* comptime
compilation, classes, captures, replace, split, `\p{L}`, `\p{Script=ŌĆ”}`, and all three byte
backends), built with Zig `0.17.0-dev` on macOS arm64:

| Optimize mode | Demo binary |
|---|---|
| `Debug` | **~3.5 MB** (3,682,968 B) |
| `ReleaseSafe` | **~1.30 MB** (1,362,504 B) |
| `ReleaseFast` | **~1.17 MB** (1,226,408 B) |
| `ReleaseSmall` | **~777 KB** (794,968 B) |

The bulk of the `Debug` figure is the Zig `Debug` runtime (DWARF self-unwind, UBSan, the
allocator), not regex data ŌĆö `ReleaseSmall` strips that down to ~777 KB, of which the shared
`ezi_code` Unicode tables (~385 KB, above) are the largest single contributor. Your own binary
will be smaller still: it won't link the demo's spread of backends and Unicode features, and
`compileRuntime` adds nothing beyond the shared tables.

```sh
zig build run                               # build + run it
zig build bench                             # benchmarks (ReleaseFast by default)
zig build test -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe       # full suite (ReleaseSafe is faster than Debug)
```

The test suite is split into **16 independently-cacheable units** ŌĆö one named module per area, so a
test binary only ever contains its own `test {}` blocks (Zig pulls a file's tests into every module
that reaches it via a *relative* import, but never across a *named*-module boundary). Editing one
file recompiles and re-runs only the unit(s) whose inputs changed; the rest stay cached. The units:
`utils`, `core`, `engine_base`, the eight backends (`backtrack`, `pikevm`, `bytepike`, `dfa`, `edfa`,
`onepass`, `literal`, `auto`), `regex`, `conformance`, `redos`, `fuzz`, and `exe`.

```sh
zig build test-core                         # run ONE unit (cached; also test-auto, test-edfa, ŌĆ”)
zig build test-conformance -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe
zig build --help                            # lists every test-<unit> step
# Gate the aggregate `test` step to a subset (REPEAT the flag ŌĆö there is no comma form):
zig build test -Dinclude-test=auto -Dinclude-test=conformance -Doptimize=ReleaseSafe
```

Use `test-<unit>` while iterating on one file; run the full `zig build test` before committing.
`zig build test` prints nothing and exits `0` on success; a failure prints the failing test.

### Fuzzing

The `fuzz` unit is a coverage-guided harness (Zig's `std.testing.fuzz` + `Smith`) over the public
API ŌĆö see [`fuzz/README.md`](fuzz/README.md). It runs **finite** by default (replays a seed corpus,
doubling as a smoke test in `zig build test`); add `--fuzz=N` for a bounded soak:

```sh
zig build fuzz                              # finite smoke run (no instrumentation)
zig build fuzz --fuzz=200000                # bounded coverage-guided session (K/M/G suffixes ok)
```

> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Bare `zig build test --fuzz` (no `=N`) fuzzes **forever** across every binary by design ŌĆö for a
> bounded run always use `--fuzz=N` and target the `fuzz` unit. Targets: scanner-never-crashes,
> cross-backend span agreement (Pike VM oracle), and exact `{m,n}`-limit accept/reject.

## Known limitations

A few deliberate semantic choices (`\X` on the backtracker only, bounded `{m,n}` counts) are
documented in [`docs/limitations.md`](docs/limitations.md). As of v0.6.0 there are **no known
cross-backend correctness gaps**: empty-width loops follow RE2/Rust leftmost-first uniformly
(`(?:|.)+` ŌåÆ `""`, `(?:a?b??)+` ŌåÆ `"a"`), and the two former deferred edge cases ŌĆö an empty
loop over a nullable concat body, and a `\b`/`\B` after a length-varying alternation ŌĆö are
fixed (see the [CHANGELOG](CHANGELOG.md), v0.6.0).

## License

MIT ŌĆö see [LICENSE](LICENSE).

releases.name: v0.5.1
releases.body:
Patch release. Leftmost-first correctness fixes for degenerate anchor / empty-width-loop / word-boundary patterns surfaced by the coverage-guided fuzz suite, plus a new `docs/limitations.md`. Results-invariant for real-world patterns ŌĆö the benchmarked `\b`/`$`/`(?m)` fast paths stay DFA-eligible and regex-bench parity is unchanged.

## Fixed

- **`(?m)` line-anchor leftmost-first divergences** ŌĆö a `line_end` immediately followed by a `line_start` over a zero-width position (`(?m:$^)`, `(?m:$$^)`), and a `(?m)` line anchor inside an alternation branch (`(?m:$)|.`). Both matched on the Pike VM but the eager byte DFA (`auto`) diverged; widened `hir.Analysis.complex_line_anchor` declines them to the Pike VM. The benchmarked leading-`(?m)^` / trailing-`(?m)$` fast paths are unaffected.
- **`\b`/`\B` with two adjacent repetitions** (`\n+(\n.*){0,2}\b`) ŌĆö an ambiguous split let the boundary hold at an early (leftmost-first) end and a later one; the leftmost-longest byte DFA took the later. New `hir.Analysis.word_boundary_with_adjacent_repetition` declines it; a single boundary-tight rep (`\b\w+\b`, `\w*\b`, `.*\b`) is unambiguous and stays DFA-routed.
- **Empty-width-loop over-consumption** (`(?:c*?){3,}.` ŌåÆ `"cc"`, `(?:a??){3,}` ŌåÆ `"aaa"`) ŌĆö an unbounded outer repetition over a nullable repetition body over-consumed on the Pike VM, matching neither Rust nor V8. Repeating a nullable repetition is idempotent up to its unbounded form (`(S*){m,} ŌēĪ S*`, `(S??){3,} ŌēĪ S*?`), so it is now collapsed in HIR (`astNullableRepBody` + `widenBodyRepToUnbounded`) ŌĆö a shared-front-end fix, so every backend agrees. Consume capability is preserved (`(?:a*?)+b` ŌåÆ `"aaab"`).

All five are validated against Rust `regex` (spans **and** capture slots) and pinned by conformance regressions with benchmark controls.

## Docs

- New [`docs/limitations.md`](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/v0.5.1/docs/limitations.md): the deliberate semantic choices (JS-style empty-loop ties, `\X` on the backtracker only, the bounded `{m,n}` ceiling) and **two deferred** edge cases on pathological patterns, each pinned by a conformance test ŌĆö an empty-width loop over a nullable *concat* body (`pikevm`/`backtrack` only; `auto` correct), and a `\b`/`\B` after a length-varying alternation (eager DFA / `auto`).

## Notes

Leftmost-first and byte-identical across backends for real-world patterns (cross-backend + comptime conformance), ReDoS-immune, runtime + comptime. ezi_gex follows JS empty-loop semantics (verified cross-engine). Min Zig 0.17.0-dev; pre-1.0 (SemVer, `@stable-since`). `main` is `0.6.0-dev`. Full detail in CHANGELOG.md and `docs/limitations.md`.

releases.name: v0.5.0
releases.body:
Feature + hardening release. The public API rounds out (full replace family + iterator/lookup helpers), a configurable `{m,n}` limit lands, and a coverage-guided fuzz suite drives six leftmost-first byte-DFA correctness fixes ŌĆö all results-invariant (regex-bench parity unchanged).

## Added

- **Replace family + iterator/lookup round-out** ŌĆö `replace` / `replaceN` / `replaceAllAlloc` / `replaceAllWith` / `capturesAt` / `splitN` / `groupIndex` / `groupName`, bringing the surface to parity with Rust `regex` / Python `re` / Go `regexp`. All `@stable-since v0.5.0`.
- **Configurable `{m,n}` repetition ceiling** (`Options.max_repetition`, default `100_000`). A finite bound past it is rejected **at scan time** with the new `quantifier_exceeds_limit` diagnostic (a DoS guard, before any lowering); also reachable on the lower layers via `scanner.Limits` ŌåÆ `scanWith` / `parseWith` / `compileWith`.
- **Coverage-guided fuzz suite** (`fuzz/`, `zig build fuzz`) ŌĆö eight `std.testing.fuzz` targets over three `Smith` generators: scanner robustness, cross-backend **span** / **capture-slot** / **`findAll`-sequence** agreement, the `{m,n}` limit, the anchor/zero-width supports gate, **Unicode** (incl. invalid UTF-8 + a `\X` no-crash target), and **`strategy`-tier results-invariance**. The bodies are finite corpus-replay smoke tests under `zig build test`; `zig build fuzz --fuzz=N` runs a bounded soak. (A complementary *cross-engine* differential against Rust `regex` lives in the sibling `regex-bench`, not in this library.)
- **More `auto` prefilters** ŌĆö a `\b`-wrapped pure-literal O(1) boundary confirm (`\bthe\b`), a fixed-offset interior-anchor confirm (`\d{4}-ŌĆ”`), and line-anchored span/capture dispatch (`log_line`).

## Changed

- **Replace runs at search speed** when the `$`-template references no captures.
- **Eager-DFA / byte-lowering build is no longer quadratic** in a Unicode class's size, plus an eager-DFA determinization budget (email compile **884 ms ŌåÆ 6.4 ms**).
- **Per-module test units** ŌĆö `zig build test` is flag-selectable and caches per module (now **16 units**, incl. the new `fuzz` unit).

## Fixed

- **Six leftmost-first byte-DFA `supports`-gate fixes**, surfaced by the new fuzz suite and an external Rust-`regex` differential: `\b`/`\B` in or beside a nullable alternation (`\b|.`, `\B(?:|.*)`); `\b`/`\B` with a lazy repetition (`a*?\b`, `[^a]+?\B *`); a repetition over a nullable alternation (`(?:|.)+`); a non-trailing `$`/`\z` (`$b$`); and a complex `(?m)` line anchor (`(?m:$\n)`). Each declines the shape to the Pike VM (correct + O(input)) and is pinned by a conformance regression whose controls keep the benchmarked `\b`/`$`/`(?m)` fast paths DFA-eligible.

## Notes

Results-invariant ŌĆö no public signature or match-result changes; the regex-bench parity suite shows **all 32 match counts unchanged and no search regression** (geomean vs Rust still 1.12├Ś). ezi_gex follows JS empty-loop semantics (verified cross-engine). Leftmost-first and byte-identical across every backend (cross-backend + comptime conformance), ReDoS-immune, runtime + comptime. Min Zig 0.17.0-dev; pre-1.0 (SemVer, `@stable-since`). `main` is now `0.6.0-dev`. Full analysis in CHANGELOG.md.

releases.name: v0.4.0
releases.body:
Feature release: the literal/prefilter fast path and the byte-DFA throughput path land ŌĆö SIMD prefilters, the eager DFA as the default span engine, and `(?m)^` line anchors on the lazy DFA. Quadratic-immune and arch-agnostic (validated on arm64, x86, and wasm).

## Added

- **SIMD prefilters** in the default `auto` engine, all results-invariant: a portable two-byte `memmem` for single literals (`Sherlock` ~40 GiB/s, ahead of Rust); the **Teddy** multi-literal prefilter for alternations, extended to the NFA/DFA arm for multi-prefix sets (`HolmesŌĆ”|WatsonŌĆ”`) and synthesised **case-variant** sets for `(?i)`/small-class leads (`(?i)the` ŌåÆ `{THEŌĆ”the}`); a **leading-class SIMD scan** (`classscan.zig`) for selective digit/number leads (`\d+`, `\p{N}+`); and an **inner-literal anchor** skip (`[\w.+-]+@ŌĆ”`).
- **Byte DFAs ŌĆö the throughput path.** The **eager DFA** (fully determinized, frozen, comptime-bakeable into `ro_data` ŌĆö the CTRE lane) with the **lazy DFA** as the big-Unicode-class fallback. `\b`/`\B` on both (ASCII on the eager, Unicode on the lazy via a decode-hybrid); anchored-end `$` and a single leading **`(?m)^`** on the lazy DFA ŌĆö the latter single-pass and quadratic-immune for the prone, newline-crossing line patterns the eager DFA declines (`log_line`).
- **One-pass NFA capture fast path** for unambiguous patterns.
- A reproducible three-engine **benchmark** ŌĆö [regex-bench](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/regex-bench): ezi_gex vs Rust `regex` vs Go `regexp` on real rebar haystacks, with a match-count correctness gate. ezi_gex is fastest in 8/32 cells and ahead of Rust on single-literal and sparse digit/number scans.

## Updated

- The **eager DFA is now the default span engine** (byte-DFA-first `auto`); captures are filled by the one-pass table or the Pike VM.
- **Hopcroft/Moore minimization** of the eager DFA ŌĆö smaller frozen transition tables.
- **Two-phase eager-DFA build** ŌĆö the unanchored `utrans` table is built only for prone patterns, so the common case keeps just its forward table.

## Fixed

- **Bounded-large-prefix anchored-restart hardening.** `computeProne` now also flags a long *bounded* prefix (`a{4000}b`), routing it to the eager DFA's O(input) native find instead of a ╬ś(n┬Ęk) per-occurrence confirm. (The ╬ś(n┬▓) dense-prefix ReDoS itself was fixed in v0.3.1.)

## Notes

Leftmost-first and byte-identical across every backend (cross-backend + comptime conformance), ReDoS-immune (dedicated suite), and validated by execution on arm64 NEON, x86 SSSE3/AVX2 (Rosetta), and wasm32 (scalar fallbacks, runtime + comptime). Min Zig 0.17.0-dev; pre-1.0 (SemVer, `@stable-since`); `main` is now `0.5.0-dev`.

**Additive and results-invariant** ŌĆö no public signature or match-result changes. Full analysis in CHANGELOG.md.

releases.name: v0.3.1
releases.body:
Patch release: fixes a quadratic-time (O(n^2)) ReDoS in the **default engine**.

- **Fixed** ŌĆö `auto`'s prefilter confirmed anchored at *every* prefix-byte occurrence, so a begin-but-don't-complete pattern with a dense prefix (e.g. `a+b` on `aaaaŌĆ”a!`) ran in **╬ś(n┬▓)** ŌĆö ~1.1 s at 64 KiB ŌĆö even though the eager DFA's own find is O(n). It now routes such programs (`prone`/`end_anchored`) to that O(n) native find (`a+b`: **1.1 s ŌåÆ 186 ┬Ąs**, now linear). Same fix on the lazy-DFA and NFA arms. Results-invariant.
- **Added** ŌĆö a ReDoS-immunity test suite (`engine/redos.zig`) with deterministic guards (`backtrack.Scratch.steps`, `auto.Scratch.confirm_probes`) that pin linear work *and* correct arm-routing, plus a `redos` benchmark ŌĆö the bench that caught this.
- **Docs** ŌĆö corrected `backtrack.zig`'s recursion-depth claim (stack depth is input-proportional; the bare backtracker is a bounded-input tool, shielded on the default path).

Additive and results-invariant ŌĆö no public signature or match-result changes. Full analysis in [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/v0.3.1/CHANGELOG.md).

releases.name: v0.3.0
releases.body:
The byte **DFA is now the default span engine**, and matching is **linear-time / ReDoS-immune on every supported pattern**.

## Highlights

- **Eager DFA (`backends.edfa`) ŌĆö the default span engine.** Fully determinizes the byte automaton at build into a frozen `states ├Ś byte_classes` table; **stateless**, **comptime-bakeable** (the CTRE lane), `O(input)` on every pattern. ~5ŌĆō10├Ś the old NFA default on character-class scans ŌĆö **at Rust-`regex` parity** (~1.1ŌĆō1.3├Ś).
- **`$`/`\z` quadratic immunity.** Trailing-`$` patterns (`\w+$`, `[ab]*c$`, `\w+@\w+$`, and `$`-in-every-branch alternations like `foo$|bar$`) match in `O(input)` via a reverse-DFA-from-end pass ŌĆö never a ╬ś(n┬▓) anchored restart. A *mixed* `$` (`a$|b`) is routed to the linear Pike VM.
- **Hash-interned determinization.** DFA-state interning is now an open-addressing hash (was `O(states┬▓)`), so big Unicode-class patterns compile in ~ms instead of ~seconds. Build-time only; no semantic change.
- **Lazy DFA fallback** (`backends.dfa`) with an `O(n)` reverse-DFA `find`, for patterns past the eager DFA's state bound.
- **Prefilter wired** (leading-literal `memchr` + rarest-required-byte fast-reject), and the **literal-alternation ╬ś(n┬▓) fixed** (single SIMD `indexOfAny` pass).

Spans and captures stay leftmost-first and conformance-pinned to the Pike VM (**311 tests**, runtime + comptime). Depends on `ezi_code` 0.4.1; minimum Zig `0.17.0-dev`.

Full notes: [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/v0.3.0/CHANGELOG.md).

releases.name: v0.2.0 ŌĆö byte-NFA lowering + ByteMap
releases.body:
Second tagged release. Pre-1.0 (SemVer; APIs annotated `@stable-since`). Requires a recent Zig `0.17.0-dev` build.

## Headline: the byte-engine substrate

- **Byte-NFA lowering + `ByteMap` equivalence classes** (`engine/byte.zig`) ŌĆö the zero-decode UTF-8 automaton substrate the lazy DFA will determinize. A scalar range lowers to UTF-8 byte-range sequences (the Cox/RE2 `utf8-ranges` algorithm, exact across length + surrogate boundaries), so a Unicode class matches the same code points with **no decode**. `byteClasses()` computes sound, contiguous byte equivalence classes (alphabet compression ŌĆö even `\w+`'s automaton collapses to ~112 classes). Builds at comptime and runtime; gated by `byteLowerable(hir)` (`\X` and `\b`/`\B` are not byte-lowerable).
- **`bytepike` backend** ŌĆö a byte-stepping Pike VM that executes the byte program (captures, comptime + runtime). The reference executor proving the lowering correct: conformance shows it agrees with `pikevm`/`backtrack`/`auto` across the whole case table, runtime and comptime. It is **not** `auto`'s default (per-byte stepping is not a throughput win ŌĆö the DFA, coming in 0.3.0, is what the substrate exists for). Invalid UTF-8 is dead-on-invalid by construction.

> **Memory note:** byte programs are *larger* than the code-point program for Unicode classes (a single `\w+` is ~137 KB vs 6.5 KB; ASCII patterns are unchanged) ŌĆö inherent to a byte NFA, and only paid when the byte path is used. The huge NFA still collapses to ~100 byte classes, which is the point: the lazy DFA keys on class id, so its alphabet stays tiny.

## Also in 0.2.0

- **Full case folding** (`CaseFold.full`) ŌĆö `(?i)├¤` also matches `ss`, `’¼Ć` matches `ff`, etc. (literals; classes stay simple-fold).
- **Grapheme `\X`** (UAX #29) ŌĆö matched by the `backtrack`/`auto` backends.
- **Two-tier `Options`** ŌĆö semantic flags (`case_fold`, `case_insensitive`/`multiline`/`dot_matches_newline` seeding, `unicode` ASCII mode) + a results-invariant `strategy` tier.
- **`(?x)` verbose mode**, **`SearchOptions.span_end`**, reserved **`Match.pattern`** (multi-pattern API), and the **`utils` facade** as the sole `ezi_code` seam.
- **Dead-on-invalid UTF-8** in input ŌĆö a match never spans a malformed byte (behaviour change vs 0.1.0's `U+FFFD` substitution). Patterns stay strictly validated.

See `CHANGELOG.md` for the full list.

## Install

```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#v0.2.0
```

```zig
const ezi_gex = b.dependency("ezi_gex", .{ .target = target, .optimize = optimize });
exe.root_module.addImport("ezi_gex", ezi_gex.module("ezi_gex"));
```

**Next (`0.3.0-dev`):** the lazy DFA over this byte substrate.

releases.name: v0.1.0 ŌĆö first tagged release
releases.body:
First public surface and first tagged release of **ezi_gex** ŌĆö a Unicode-aware regex engine for Zig that runs at **runtime and comptime**, behind a pluggable, vtable-free backend contract.

### Highlights
- **Pipeline** `pattern ŌåÆ AST ŌåÆ HIR ŌåÆ Program ŌåÆ match`, runnable at runtime (heap) and comptime (program baked into `ro_data`, match runs in const-eval).
- **Backends:** `auto` (default dispatcher), `pikevm` (Pike VM, captures, linear-time), `backtrack` (bounded, memoized, linear-time), `literal` (substring / literal-alternation).
- **Tier-1 prefilter wired (new):** the `literal` backend scans with `std.mem.indexOf` (SIMD `memchr` + BoyerŌĆōMooreŌĆōHorspool) ŌĆö ~20├Ś on memchr-friendly needles, never slower; and `auto` consumes the HIR `Analysis` on NFA patterns ŌĆö a leading-literal first-byte `memchr` prefilter, a `^`/`\A` start short-circuit, and a `min_utf8_len` length gate. Every fact is a sound one-sided bound, so no real match is ever dropped.
- **Unicode-first:** classes, scripts, `\w`/`\b`, and simple case folding resolve to code-point ranges with **zero Unicode-table lookups at match time** (via [`ezi_code`](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-code); `ezi_gex` never touches `std.unicode`).
- **Target-agnostic library:** pure computation over caller-provided memory ŌĆö verified to compile for `wasm32-freestanding`, `wasm32-wasi`, `riscv64-freestanding`, and `aarch64-linux`.

Everything in the public surface is annotated `@stable-since: v0.1.0` and is covered by SemVer from this tag on. Requires a recent Zig `0.17.0-dev` build.

### Install
```sh
zig fetch --save git+https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex.git#v0.1.0
# or, plain HTTP tarball (pins the content hash):
zig fetch --save https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/archive/refs/tags/v0.1.0.tar.gz
```

See [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/shaik-abdul-thouhid/ezi-gex/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md) for the full list.
